datapath.summary: SRT-4 radix-4 integer divider with carry-save (CSA) iteration loop and 68-bit PR width. The datapath has four major phases: (1) PREPARE — input absolute value, leading-zero count, barrel-shift normalization, (2) ITERATE — redundant PR shift-by-2 + QDS + mult select + 3:2 CSA compression, repeated N times, (3) CORRECT — 68-bit CPA merge of sum+carry, then sign correction and PR-based remainder recovery, (4) OUTPUT — sign application and handshake. The critical innovation is the CSA iteration loop: replacing a 68-bit CPA with XOR/AND gates reduces the iteration cycle from ~6.2ns to <0.5ns at 2GHz.

storage_elements.name: abs_a_q / abs_d_q
storage_elements.description: Latched absolute values of dividend and divisor, captured on PREPARE. Hold steady through ITERATE/CORRECT for sign application in OUTPUT.

storage_elements.name: d_norm_q
storage_elements.description: Normalized divisor: |D| << lzc_d, with MSB at bit 63. Used: (a) d_hat (bits[62:60]) for QDS, (b) D_scaled derivation for mult mux, (c) R recovery in K-odd path (bit[0]×D_norm addback).

storage_elements.name: pr_sum_q / pr_carry_q
storage_elements.description: Redundant partial remainder (carry-save representation). pr_sum_q holds XOR result, pr_carry_q holds AND+left-shifted carry (LSB always 0). Together: actual PR = pr_sum_q + pr_carry_q. Updated every ITERATE cycle. Initialized to {4'b0, A_norm} and 0 on PREPARE.

storage_elements.name: pr_full_q
storage_elements.description: Non-redundant PR after full 68-bit CPA merge (pr_sum_q + pr_carry_q). Latched in CORRECT stage, consumed in CORRECT2+OUTPUT.

storage_elements.name: Q_q / QM_q
storage_elements.description: On-the-fly quotient accumulators. QM = Q - 1 always invariant. Updated every ITERATE cycle via OTF conversion rules (per q_digit). In CORRECT2: if PR negative, select QM (quotient was 1 too large).

storage_elements.name: n_q / shift_q / lzc_d_q / iter_cnt_q
storage_elements.description: Control registers: N (iteration count), shift (quotient realignment {0,1}), lzc_d (for R recovery barrel shift), iter_cnt (current iteration index). latched in PREPARE, iter_cnt auto-increments in ITERATE.

storage_elements.name: dividend_q / divisor_q / signed_op_q
storage_elements.description: Input operand registers. Latched from input pins on IDLE→PREPARE transition (valid_i & ready_o). Used throughout operation for sign control and short-circuit detection.

storage_elements.name: quotient_o_q / remainder_o_q
storage_elements.description: Output data registers. Updated in OUTPUT state with sign-corrected result. Held until next OUTPUT or reset.

storage_elements.name: div_by_zero_q / overflow_q
storage_elements.description: Output exception flags. Updated alongside quotient/remainder in OUTPUT state. Held until next operation or reset.

datapath.buffering_behavior: No FIFO or data buffering. Single-operation, non-pipelined: one set of operand registers captures the current operation; new inputs while busy are ignored (ready_o=0). Output registers hold results until consumed (ready_i handshake) or overwritten by next operation.
datapath.data_flow: Input: pins → IDLE latch → [abs → CLZ → norm → PREPARE regs] (combo in state) → [ITERATE loop: <<2 → 7-bit CPA → QDS → mult mux → CSA 3:2 → regs] × N → [CORRECT: 68-bit CPA → pr_full reg] → [CORRECT2: pr_full → sign select → Q shift → PR-based R recovery] → [OUTPUT: apply_sign → output regs] → pins.

control.summary: 6-state FSM governing pipeline stages IDLE → PREPARE → ITERATE → CORRECT → CORRECT2 → OUTPUT. Each state maps to a distinct datapath phase. Iteration is variable-length (N rounds, N determined by operand magnitude). Short-circuit conditions bypass ITERATE+CORRECT entirely (PREPARE→OUTPUT). Output handshake (valid_o/ready_i) controls return to IDLE.

fsm_list.name: div_srt_fsm
fsm_list.key_transitions: IDLE→PREPARE: valid_i & ready_o. PREPARE→OUTPUT (short-circuit) or →ITERATE (normal). ITERATE→ITERATE (N-1 times, dp_done=0). ITERATE→CORRECT (dp_done=1). CORRECT→CORRECT2 (always). CORRECT2→OUTPUT (always). OUTPUT→IDLE (ready_i). No self-loop on OUTPUT (must consume before next op). All other transitions are unconditional.

control.scheduling: Non-pipelined single-issue. FSM issues control pulses (prep_step, iter_step, corr_step) one at a time. No arbitration or out-of-order completion. The ITERATE state issues iter_step each cycle and relies on the datapath's combinational done_o signal to detect loop termination.
control.update_ordering: (1) PREPARE → operand latching happens in IDLE→PREPARE transition (state update before combinational output calc). (2) All ITERATE updates (PR, Q/QM, iter_cnt) happen simultaneously on each clock edge. (3) CORRECT pr_full merges pr_sum+pr_carry; CORRECT2 consumes pr_full to compute Q_int+R combinationaly. (4) OUTPUT updates output registers with sign-corrected results. Ordering is strictly sequential with no data hazards between states.

performance_constraints.throughput: 1 operation per (5 + N_min_to_max) cycles, where N ∈ [1, 32]. Worst-case throughput: 1 op / 37 cycles. No pipelining — back-to-back operations must wait for full completion + handshake.
performance_constraints.latency: Short-circuit: 3 cycles (1.2 ns @ 2.5 GHz). |D|=1: 4 cycles (1.6 ns). Normal min (N=1): 6 cycles (2.4 ns). Normal max (N=32): 37 cycles (14.8 ns). Average (random 64-bit operands, uniform K distribution): ~19 cycles (7.6 ns).
performance_constraints.folding_factor: 1 operation at a time (no temporal folding). CSA iteration is fully unfolded within the serial loop.

low_power.clock_gating: Not implemented. All sequential elements receive un-gated clock. Significant power optimization opportunity: operand registers, Q/QM, and PR registers only need updates during specific states. Clock gating could reduce dynamic power by ~60-70% during IDLE periods.

power_domains.name: vdd_core
power_domains.description: Single power domain. No power gating or retention strategy specified.

low_power.retention_requirements: No retention specified. Since the module has no configuration state, power-on reset reinitializes everything. If integrated into a CPU with power gating, the divider can be fully reset on wake.

hazards_and_ordering.description: Output handshake (valid_o/ready_i): when valid_o=1 and ready_i=0, OUTPUT state stalls. Results remain stable on output registers but the module is busy and cannot accept new inputs. No timeout or watchdog mechanism.
hazards_and_ordering.resolution: Standard stall — downstream consumer must assert ready_i to complete. No deadlock as long as consumer eventually accepts.

hazards_and_ordering.description: Input handshake (valid_i/ready_o): when valid_i arrives during non-IDLE state, ready_o=0 and the input is ignored. The module does not queue or buffer pending requests.
hazards_and_ordering.resolution: CPU scheduler must ensure valid_i is not asserted until ready_o=1 (or tolerate dropped requests).

hazards_and_ordering.description: No read-after-write (RAW) or write-after-write (WAW) hazards within the iteration loop — iteration i writes PR/Q/QM, and iteration i+1 reads the just-written values on the next clock edge. This is the intended sequential behavior.
hazards_and_ordering.resolution: A single cycle of register delay between iterations is correct by design for the SRT algorithm.

verification_sensitive.why_sensitive: The FSM resets to IDLE, but PR_sum/carry, Q/QM, and iter_cnt all clear to zero. If reset asserts while pr_full_q is partially computed in CORRECT, the next operation starts clean. Need to verify no X-propagation or metastability on the reset-release edge.
verification_sensitive.suggested_coverage: Reset assertion during each FSM state; reset release timing relative to clock edge.

verification_sensitive.why_sensitive: Five short-circuit conditions have a defined priority (D=0 > INT64_MIN/-1 > A=0 > |A|<|D| > |D|=1). If multiple conditions are true simultaneously (e.g., D=0 && A=0), the priority encoding must select the correct result. The current logic uses separate if-else in OUTPUT state — priority is implicit in the evaluation order.
verification_sensitive.suggested_coverage: All 5!/(5-2)! = 20 pairwise combinations of simultaneous short-circuit conditions.

verification_sensitive.behavior: K parity boundary: shift=0 vs shift=1
verification_sensitive.why_sensitive: The R recovery formula differs for K-even (shift=0, direct >>(lzc_d+2)) and K-odd (shift=1, ((>>2) + b*D_norm) >>(lzc_d+1)). The `q_corrected[0]` bit feeds a conditional add. Any error in this branch affects every K-odd operation (~50% of random inputs).
verification_sensitive.suggested_coverage: Systematic K = even/odd cross-product at K=0,1,2,3,61,62 boundaries.

verification_sensitive.behavior: PR sign correction boundary: exactly zero
verification_sensitive.why_sensitive: PR correction logic: if pr_negative (pr_full[67]=1), add D_scaled and select QM. If pr_full is exactly zero (pr_full=0), pr_negative=0, no correction applied. The boundary case where PR is negative but near-zero requires careful checking: does pr_full+ D_scaled ever overflow? Is Q/QM selection always correct?
verification_sensitive.suggested_coverage: Directed tests where expected PR_N is zero or near-zero after N iterations.

verification_sensitive.why_sensitive: QDS selection uses pr_est >= M2/M1/-M1/-M2. When pr_est exactly equals a threshold, the selection is at a decision boundary. The 7-bit CPA for pr_est has rounding error (low-order bits truncated), so the boundary behavior must be tolerant.
verification_sensitive.suggested_coverage: Dither pr_est targeting each M{1,2} ±1 threshold for all 8 d_hat values (8×4×2 = 64 boundary tests per iteration).

verification_sensitive.why_sensitive: The PR_0 = {4'b0, A_norm} fix specifically targets the case where dividend[63]=1 in unsigned mode. The old PR_0 = A_norm>>1 lost the LSB in this case. Must ensure this fix is never regressed.
verification_sensitive.suggested_coverage: Regression test: unsigned dividend with MSB=1 and bit[0]=1 (odd).

architecture_risks.risk: CORRECT 68-bit CPA timing at 2.5 GHz: First synthesis (2 GHz, RVT xp33) showed WNS = -6836 ps for the CORRECT CPA path. At 2.5 GHz the slack worsens to ~-6936 ps. The 68-bit CPA in CORRECT stage is currently a single-cycle operation. If it cannot meet 400 ps timing with SLVT/larger cells, it needs to be split into 2+ cycles (adding 1-2 cycles to every division).
architecture_risks.mitigation: 1) Target re-synthesis with ASAP7 SLVT cells + larger drive strengths. 2) Consider splitting the 68-bit CPA into sub-adder groups with pipeline register. 3) Carry-lookahead or carry-select adder architecture instead of ripple-carry from ABC.

architecture_risks.risk: No clock gating: In a CPU execution unit, the divider is idle most of the time (divide instructions are infrequent). Without clock gating, all ~550 flops toggle every cycle, wasting dynamic power. 37-cycle latency × high toggle rate during iteration makes this a significant power hotspot.
architecture_risks.mitigation: Add state-based clock gating: (a) disable PR/Q/QM clocks during IDLE and OUTPUT, (b) disable operand register clocks after PREPARE, (c) disable output register clocks after handshake completes. Use integrated clock gate (ICG) cells for synthesis.

architecture_risks.risk: SRT-4 to SRT-8 upgrade: SRT-8 would need pr_est width of ~8-9 bits (vs 7), QDS table of ~9×4 = 4096 entries (vs 8×128 implicit), different D_scaled alignment, and ρ re-evaluation. The existing FSM, handshake, and control infrastructure is reusable, but the datapath (PR width, mult mux, QDS) is SRT-4 specific.
architecture_risks.mitigation: Decide in Phase 2: either (a) keep SRT-4 and SRT-8 as separate modules sharing only the FSM/control, or (b) design a parameterized datapath that configures for radix-4 or radix-8 at compile time.

architecture_risks.risk: Bus saturation under backpressure: If the downstream CPU core stalls (ready_i=0) while the divider is in OUTPUT, the module holds results indefinitely. This is safe but means a single slow consumer can block the entire divider pipeline. With no queuing, this is a performance bottleneck for bursty divide workloads.
architecture_risks.mitigation: Accept as-designed for single-issue CPU. For out-of-order cores, consider adding a result queue (FIFO) to decouple divider completion from consumer readiness.
